title: 2.4 GHz Flip-flop Device within Nonequilibrium Superconducting Diode
authors: Xiangyu Bi, Hongyi Li, Aoshen Yang, Yuqiang Fang, Ganyu Chen, Shichong Yang, Yicheng Shen, Qizheng Sun, Junwei Huang, Wei Jiang, Da Wang, Fuqiang Huang, Haijun Zhang, Qianghua Wang, Hongtao Yuan
abstract: Superconducting diode effect exhibits asymmetric critical supercurrent and has profound implications for condensed matter physics. The technical appeals of such superconducting diodes are their ultrahigh on-off ratio and diode efficiency for superconducting electronics owing to the dissipationless supercurrent therein. However, realizing superconducting diode operation at high working frequency, which is a key requirement for practical applications, remains elusive and challenging. Here, we demonstrate a polarity-controllable superconducting diode with non-equilibrium Josephson junction and its edge-triggered flip-flop operation at a high frequency up to 2.4 GHz, within a van der Waals superconductor 2M-WS$_2$. By simply tuning the thickness of superconducting 2M-WS$_2$ nanoflakes to engineer inversion asymmetry in the junction, we achieve a high diode efficiency of 67% and an on-off ratio exceeding 10$^5$. Importantly, the pulse width and duty cycle of output pulse signals in such superconducting diode flip-flop devices can be controlled in a broadband frequency range crossing 12 orders of magnitude. Theoretical analysis reveals that the non-equilibrium dynamic nature of supercurrent in these Josephson junctions enables such a high diode operating frequency and the polarity control of supercurrent. The 2.4 GHz non-equilibrium Josephson diode developed here provides a promising platform for advanced superconducting logic circuits and broadband telecommunication applications.
summary: This paper reports on the realization of a highly efficient, field-free superconducting diode operating at 2.4 GHz using van der Waals superconductors. By engineering junction asymmetry, they achieved robust rectification and demonstrated its application in an edge-triggered flip-flop. This work provides a promising platform for next-generation superconducting logic circuits.
main_problem: The primary challenge addressed is achieving high-frequency, field-free superconducting diode operation for practical superconducting electronics.
main_result: The authors demonstrated a polarity-controllable superconducting diode flip-flop operating up to 2.4 GHz using van der Waals superconductors, exhibiting high efficiency and an on-off ratio exceeding $10^5$.
method: The work combines experimental measurements (I-V curves, high-frequency pulse generation) with theoretical modeling using the Resistively Shunted Junction (RCSJ) model.
model_or_system: The physical system utilizes Josephson junctions formed in van der Waals superconductor heterojunctions ($	ext{2M-WS}_2/	ext{NbSe}_2$). The underlying mechanism is explained by non-equilibrium dynamics and geometric inversion asymmetry, which allows for diode behavior without breaking time-reversal symmetry.
key_observables:
Diode efficiency ($\eta$), on-off ratio ($>10^5$), operating frequency (up to 2.4 GHz), and measurable supercurrent asymmetry ($I_{c+} 
eq |I_{c-}|$).
important_parameters: Thickness tuning of $	ext{2M-WS}_2$ nanoflakes, junction geometry, input signal waveform (sine, triangle, chainsaw).
main_assumptions: The nonreciprocal effect is robustly explained by the nonlinear quantum capacitance mechanism within the RCSJ framework, which accounts for noise and geometric asymmetry.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate I-V characteristics confirming Josephson junctions, compare diode efficiency across different geometries, and show the output pulse signal generation from the flip-flop device at high frequencies.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the feasibility of field-free superconducting diodes using $	ext{WS}_2$ heterojunctions, then detailing the mechanism via RCSJ modeling, and finally demonstrating the practical application through high-frequency edge-triggered flip-flop operation up to 2.4 GHz.
why_interesting: The work bridges condensed matter physics (superconductivity) with device engineering by showing how fundamental quantum effects (non-equilibrium dynamics) can be harnessed for high-speed classical electronic components, relevant to superconducting circuit design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
